sentences.text: Well, it became more noticeable in the school.
The first thing the teachers did-- the first thing that happened-- some of the teachers, especially some that I liked, particularly, were dismissed.
They were members of the Catholic party.
They were members of the Social Democratic Party.
And they were simply dismissed.
And they were-- they appointed teachers who were outright Nazis.
I remember one, especially, was our music teacher.
We had an excellent music teacher, but he was thrown out of school.
And the fellow who was in charge of the stormtroopers band, you know, the guy that marches in front with the baton, he, all of a sudden, became our music teacher.
And he was a vicious anti-Semite.
And in his classroom, it started where he immediately separated all the Jewish students-- there were 11 of us in that class-- he separated us from all the other students.
We had to sit in the back of the classroom, and the Gentiles in front.
He never called on us.
He never taught us anything, except called us names.
And when-- when it came time for a report card, he failed each and every one of us.
Even so one of my friends was a good piano player, it didn't make any difference.
We all failed.
So-- but that kind of separation between the Jewish and the Gentile students then also happened in the other classrooms.
And we could tell, from one month to the next, that it became more and more difficult for us Jewish students to attend classes.
And many, many of them dropped out.
I think I was among the last three or four who stayed in school.
Oh, yeah.
There were only boys.
The girls went to something called [GERMAN], which is a girl's high school.
The boys went to the gymnasiums, either the one I went to, which was called [GERMAN] Gymnasium, or the one-- to the regular gymnasium.
They were similar.
Yeah.
There were no girls.
No, not a one.
Not a one.
Absolutely not.
There was, I would say, very civil relations between our neighbors and ourself, the Gentile neighbors.
But we had known them for a longer period of time.
But I noticed, even there, the two boys they had-- and they were nice boys.
We played with them in-- in-- outside, many games that children play-- they also, more or less, refrained from continuing this friendship, as the years went by.
Yeah.
Well, my father-- my father was-- it was-- it was horrible.
My father had built up a very substantial business.
And I must say, by probably any standards, particularly German standards, he was rather well off.
We lived very nicely.
We had a maid.
We had a-- a cleaning woman.
We had a governess.
We had a chauffeur, who drove us around.
And in those days, we already had a Mercedes Benz.
So we were-- we were raised as-- as affluent children in the-- in a middle class German home.
And the Nazis then appointed someone to oversee my father's business.
In German, they called him an abwickler.
That man sat in my father's office-- I see him to this very day--
Yes.
This was already the linen-- Correct.
The company was known as Hudes and Company, H-U-D-E-S in Gelsenkirchen.
But he had ties with the other manufacturers in Bielefeld and other towns.
And he distributed linens almost throughout in Germany.
He had salesman which he had hired, who did this for him.
And he and my mother, who worked in the office, they were hardworking people, both of them.
And-- and as I said, he had built up a good business.
And then, this man who came in, appointed by the Nazi, with a big swastika always on his lapel-- he wore it every morning when he came in-- his name was Novak.
And he ran my father's business, in as much as-- particularly the accounts receivables.
He saw to it that they were channeled into different funds.
And slowly, but surely, my father's business was choked, more and more, because he continued to work in a-- on a limit-- more limited basis.
But the monies that were due him, or the monies that came in, were actually stolen from him by this Novak, on behalf of the German government, the Nazi party, whatever.
That happened to many businesses owned by German-Jews.
And that's-- towards the end, there probably was very little left.
Well, it did him-- did him little good.
[CLEARS THROAT] You could protest, and I'm sure he did.
He raised his voice probably more than once.
But you had to be careful, too.
This-- these-- the-- the laws were doctored, doctored so this became law.
And he-- very heavy extra taxes were leveled against the German-Jews.
And all these things were then, so-called, quote unquote, lawful in the eyes of the German Nazi government.
And they just simply drained the business, until, more or less, towards the end, there was very little left of it.
I-- I remember, maybe not April 1, I remember Hitler's birthday, April 30th, 1933.
Why do I remember that?
We lived in an apartment, a lovely-- we had just a new building we had moved into.
And we had a sun parlor.
And I opened a window, and I heard music.
And there came a band of stormtroopers with a-- a band down the-- marching down the street.
And they were singing a song in German.
For those who, again, who speak German, the name of the song was, unfortunately, familiar to German-Jews.
And it said, [SPEAKING GERMAN], which means, when Jewish blood spurts from the knives.
And they sang this song, marching outside of our apartment.
And I was horrified.
And I ran to my dad, and I said, Dad, Dad, come here.
You have to listen to this.
Listen to what they have to sing, what they're singing.
And my dad nonchalantly walked over to the window.
He listened to it.
And he said, don't worry about it.
They will come and go, just like all the rest of them.
This was in April of '33.
And Hitler had come to power in January of that year.
And he truly believed that this was just a passing phase in the German government.
I was horrified.
So that's the day I remember, absolutely.
There were many other dates, but that in-- that one, in particular, sticks out in my mind.
I would think, yeah, she probably did.
She was not, at that moment, at the window with my dad, but she shared the same attitude.
Yes.
Her parents also had a-- a department store.
She came from a city called Norden, which is in the northern part of Germany, N-O-R-D-E-N.
And her parents had a very successful department store in that town by the name of Hartog, H-A-R-T-O-G, very well-known.
Yes, that was her maiden name.
And they had-- they were-- she came from an affluent background.
So all these German-Jews that had that kind of a background felt the same way, really, mm-hm, initially.
I-- I would-- I would.
I would think that the-- the-- the-- our parents did.

I think we felt it-- we children felt it more acutely, because of the harassment that we received almost on a daily basis, in school and on the street, and on the-- on a different level than the parents.
The parents thought, initially, that this would blow over, you see?
Until more and more laws were-- the Nuremberg laws and other laws were introduced, which made it more difficult.
We couldn't have the same maid in the household anymore.
We couldn't buy in certain stores any-- we couldn't go to a swimming pool anymore.
We couldn't go into parks anymore.
When all of these laws were introduced, we really felt it more and more, year after year.
That's correct.
That's correct.
That's what I'm talking about.
Yes, that's correct.
Mm-hm.
Also, I think, the governess at the time was younger.
Mm-hm.
Oh, absolutely.
You know, the chauffeur he had for many years, he had to dismiss.
I remember that.

And probably the obstacles he found in business, because he might have been calling on-- certainly, he did call on Gentile customers, and-- and some would no longer buy from him, because he was a Jew.
And they knew that.
Even so, he-- they treated-- he treated them, and they treated him very well, prior to Hitler coming to power.
And they had a very good relationship, good business relationship.
But suddenly, all these things changed.
Oh, yes.
People stopped buying.
Absolutely.
Yes, but no more-- I got no more beatings than kids-- other kids-- you know, give other kids on the street when they-- they punch you, or they try to box you down, or-- or hit you, or knock you down.
There were minor skirmishes, many of them, but they were minor.
Somebody came up from behind you and called you a dirty Jew, and gave you a shove or a push, or knocked you down, as I said.
They were minor, until after the 9th of November.
And maybe you want to talk about that now or later, but that was a different story.
I do.
Do you remember anything about the Olympics [INAUDIBLE]?
Only that we-- we watched-- no, we didn't watch.
There was no television in those days.
We-- we listened to it, and we read about it in the newspapers, but it didn't have much of an impact on me at all.
No.

I would just-- I just followed who won and how many gold medals each country won, but outside of that-- and we were somewhat interested, because initially, we were told that German-Jews were going to participate in-- on the German side.
But it turned out, I think, at the last minute, they withdrew the Jews and would not let them participate.
But that's-- somebody else is-- is more knowledgeable about that than I am.
But I-- I'm quite sure that I remember that.
They-- they pulled out.
They did not participate.
And they-- they had left earlier.
I'm not sure.
It was either '36 or '37.
My aunt and uncle got out before we did.
And they, again, went to relatives who already lived in Chicago.
So their name, my aunt and uncle, they were the Rothschilds.
My Aunt Erma and Uncle Fritz, and my cousin Heinz, Henry, and-- and Helga, they all came to Chicago before we did.
That was my father's sister.
That's correct.
They wrote to the parents that it was high time for us to get out of Germany.
And I think my father realized it was crucial that we would get out.
And it was natural, because we had relatives in the States, and really, nowhere else, that we would try to come to the United States.
But the laws-- the United States laws, immigration laws, made it extremely difficult for us to get out.
And that's why so many more people perished, because of the quota system.
And we were among the lucky ones who-- who got a visa.
No.
I never heard Palestine mentioned as a destination.
In the end, I think, if we could not have gotten visas to the States, my parents probably would have gone anywhere, in the end, like many people did.
People went everywhere and anywhere that would accept them.
Sometimes, they had to bribe.
They often bribed.
Particular South American councils took-- took bribes and gave people visas.
Some people got out by way of Shanghai.
But they probably would have gone anywhere, if the visa had not materialized.
They had none.
They had none.
Nope, they did not.
Well, they are among my most vivid memories of being raised in Germany.
On the 9th of November, 1938, I was an apprentice in Cologne.
And apprentice in the tailor shop in Cologne.
I came to work in the morning after an assassination of an embassy employee in-- in Paris.
And as I walked in the door, my-- my boss, who was a Gentile, married to a Jewish woman, my boss said, Herman, I don't think you ought to stay here today.
I said, why not?
He said, well, during the night, there were some pogroms against Jews in the city of Cologne.
To the best of my knowledge, they set fire to part of the synagogue.
And since everyone in-- in Germany always had to register, the German police knew where anyone was at any given time.
And they certainly knew where I lived and where I worked.

And he said, I don't think it's a good idea that you are going to be here today, because if they come to arrest you-- and I heard there have already been some arrests-- if they come to arrest you, you're just simply not here.
Now, you better leave.
And I left.
I walked out.
And I walked the streets of Cologne.
The first thing I did, I went over to the synagogue.

Well, I got within one block of the synagogue, and I saw the talismans and the prayer books laying on the street-- I think some of them were-- were even burning-- and people are milling around the synagogues who were not Jewish people, believe me.
And I was concerned to get closer.
And obviously, what my boss had told me was correct.
And I turned around, and I walked away.
I walked down the streets of Cologne.
And there's a very famous street called the [?
Ringe Strasse, ?] which is around-- it goes-- circles around Cologne.
And as I walked along, there were truckloads of stormtroopers coming up and down the ring, with German flags in the truck, yelling anti-Semitic slogans.
And all of a sudden, a truck stopped in front of me, and all the Stormtroopers piled out.
And I stood like I was glued to the sidewalk.
I didn't look particularly Jewish, so I-- I wasn't-- I was afraid, and I was not afraid, because no-- this was not the town I was born and raised in, so I didn't think anybody would know me.
They piled out and walked right into a clothing store, which was owned by a Jewish family.
I even remember the name of the store to this day.
It was called Haus der Minden.
They piled into that store.
They had, like, steel rods with them and wooden planks.
And they smashed the windows in the store, and they dragged the clothing out.
And one stormtrooper would take one pants leg, another one would take another pants leg, and they would rip these suits, the-- the two sleeves apart, two pants apart.
And they threw it all out.
And more and more people gathered around me.
As a matter-- there was a German streetcar conductor next to me.
He said, [SPEAKING GERMAN].
He-- he said, they're crazy.
They should give this to poor people.
They shouldn't tear it apart, he said.
He didn't-- the logic in his mind was not that they did this to the Jews.
That, he probably didn't even to object to.
The very fact he objected that they were destroying something he would have liked to have had, rather than have it destroyed.
Well then, slowly, I [? ebbed ?] out of the crowd, and I went to the next telephone.
And I called my parents at home.
And my mother was a bit panicky-- my stepmother that-- of course, what I called her mother, of course.
And she said, you better come home, immediately.
Take the first train, and come home.
Your dad is missing.
We don't know where he is.
They broke into the house this morning.
And she was telling me what happened when the SS invaded our home in-- in Gelsenkirchen, and that my dad had disappeared, and you better come home.
So I took the first train.
I-- I went back.
I lived in a rooming house, a Jewish boarding house, [GERMAN].
Packed my suitcase, took the first train from Cologne to Gelsenkirchen, and went home.
My father was missing.

He had received a call during the night from his brother, Alfred, who had a furniture store in a nearby town called Bottrop, that the Nazis had invaded his home and store and had set the furniture store on fire.
And he was panicky.
And my dad drove from the city of Gelsenkirchen to Bottrop to see if he could help his brother.
When he got there, he saw that the furniture store was on fire.
And he tried to return to his car, because there were-- the place was surrounded with SS and others.

And as he went to the car, which he had just bought, a new Mercedes-- he had just bought it, because he thought he could take something material out of Germany, and they told him no money, but you can take a car.
But that's all nonsense.
And as he tried to return to the car, the SS spotted him.
And one of the men knew my dad, and said there goes the Jew Cohn's brother.
And they ran after him.
And he ran into a nursery.
In the back of the nursery, they had a pit in the back where they accumulated leaves, which they then turned into fertilizer.
And he jumped into this pit and covered himself with these leaves.
And he stayed in that pit, until sun up, until the sun came up.
They even-- he even heard the dogs and the SS looking for him.
They were surrounding all over.
Everybody was looking for him.
He didn't dare, but there was enough moisture in there, so he could breathe enough oxygen.
He didn't have to come out of the leaves.
And finally, at dawn, he came out of the leaves.
And he went to look for his car.
The car had been stolen, was gone.
So he took a train home to Gelsenkirchen.
And we lived on the street with somewhat of an incline.
And as he walked up the street, the SS, who had gone to our home, was coming out of our apartment.
And they spotted my dad on the bottom of the hill.
And they ran.
And of course, by that time, my father-- he was exhausted.
He couldn't run anymore.
And they-- they beat him to a pulp and left him laying in the gutter.
Well, we didn't know this.
My mother didn't know it, my grandmother who lived with us.
Nobody knew what happened to him.
And he-- he didn't come home.
And then, I came home in the afternoon, and he was missing.
Well, the next day, we had a call that he had-- was in a hospital in Essen.
They wouldn't admit him in the hospital in Gelsenkirchen, because he was a Jew.
And they knew him.
So somebody-- we don't even know, to this day, who-- took him to Essen where they had some major hospitals.
And there he was for several days.
Now, my folks realized, at that time, the urgency of leaving Germany.
And we had all registered for visas to go to the United States.
But as I-- as I indicated earlier, there was a waiting period.
And we probably had to wait at least another year, until our number, they called it, our number came up at the German-- at the American consulate, so we could get our visa.
But my parents, who had sent my brother earlier on, a year prior, to the United States on a children's transport, kindertransport, then said, you have to get out, no matter what.
Because I was 17 years of age, and they were picking up boys my age.
They were picking them up on the streets.
And you never saw them again.
They were gone.
You can't stay here.
They know you are here.
You can't stay here.
You've got to get out.
So they made efforts and were able to get me on a train that left with the-- this kindertransport also to Holland, with all children under the age of 18.

But prior to that, I had to obtain, from the German government, releases that all my taxes were paid, my social security was paid, all the documents that you needed in order to get a visa to leave Germany.
So I-- I went to the tax bureau, the [GERMAN], they called it.
I went to the social security office.
And as I walked into the social security office, which is called the [GERMAN] in German, there was a young man my age behind the counter.
And as I walked up to him, he said, hey, aren't you Herman Cohn?
I said, yes.
And I recognized him, also.
Earlier on, he had been a classmate of mine in-- in high school, but he had dropped out for some-- whatever reason.
He was no longer in high school.
And he was working there as an employee at-- at the social security office.
He might have been 18 years of age.
I don't know-- 17, 18, whatever.
He was my age.
And I told him that I needed something stamped, and approval that my social security had been paid, in order to get a visa to leave Germany.
And he said OK, I will get it for you.
But I want to ask you something.
I said, what do you want to ask me?
He said, I heard a rumor that they set the synagogue on fire.
Is that true?
And my reply was, yes, of course it's true.
I said, everybody in town knows about it.
They set the synagogue on fire.
And the fire department didn't come, until the synagogue was practically burned down.
And then they-- they tried to extinguish the flames, but it was too late.
Our synagogue in Gelsenkirchen was absolutely burned to the ground.
He said, OK.
You wait here a few minutes.
I'll be back.
I have the stamp.
I'll have this thing stamped, so you can get your visa.
And he went to the second floor, where the head of the department had his office.
But it took him a while.
It took him almost 20 minutes.
And I was getting very uneasy.
I didn't know why he didn't come back.
And then, all of a sudden, he did come back.
And he said, the boss wants to see you.
So here are your papers.
And he handed me the papers.
And I went up with the papers to the second floor.
And there was a gentleman I didn't know sitting behind a desk.
And another man was sitting in a chair across from the desk with high boots on.
He wasn't a-- he was not in a uniform.
He had-- wore a suit, but he had these high, black boots.
You could see them coming out from under his pants.
Those were the kind of boots that were normally worn only by the SS or stormtroopers.
And as I walked in, and he said-- this man, the head of the social security office, spoke my name.
Are you Herman Cohn?
I said, yes.
He said, do you know this man?
With him.
With that, he pointed at the-- at the man seated across from him.
And he says, [SPEAKING GERMAN].
That's German for he said, do you know who that is?
And I said, I have no idea.
He said-- and he mentioned his name.
And to this day, my memory is not that good anymore.
I-- I don't quite remember the man's name.
But the moment he spoke his name, I knew who it was.
He was the head of the Gestapo in Gelsenkirchen.
It just shot through me.
I got all hot and bothered.
I knew exactly who he was.
I had never met the man, but every Jew in town had heard his name.
And here was this man, the head of the Gestapo in Gelsenkirchen, seated across from the guy who ran the social security office.
He said in German, what kind of stories did you tell your friend down there?
I said, what do you mean stories?
He says, [GERMAN].
[GERMAN] means rumors.
And I said, I didn't tell him any [GERMAN].
I didn't tell him any rumors.
Well, you told him that we set the synagogue on fire.
I said, well, you did.
I mean, everybody knows it, is you-- that the-- the SR or SS came and set the synagogue on fire.
Everybody knew it.
And the fire department didn't come, until the fire was out.
The synagogue was burned down.
I told him that, just the way I tell you this now, without any problem.
That's how it was.
He said, we are going to teach you how to tell the truth, and not how to tell lies.
We will teach you, he said, how to tell the truth.
You are not going to ever tell any more [GERMAN], rumors.
You're not ever going to tell any more rumors.
With that, he got up.
And he said, now, you're going to march to Gestapo headquarters.
And I'm going to walk five feet behind you.
Or [GERMAN], in German, he said.
And if you walk six meters in front of me, I will shoot you for trying to escape.
I just want you to know that.
You keep your distance.
You don't run, or you don't walk faster, because if you do, I will kill you for trying to escape.
Well, I knew my way to the Gestapo.
Even so I had never been there, but every Jew in town knew where the Gestapo was.
It was on the second floor of the city hall.
And I walked around the street.
And I want to tell you, I was like a zombie at that point.
I mean, I-- I wasn't even thinking, I was just walking.
And I kept the pace, so he would not find an excuse to shoot me.
I went up to the second headquarter-- second floor.
And there was a big steel door at the Gestapo headquarters.
And when that door shut after me, I thought I would never, ever come out alive.
He stood me in the corner, facing the wall.
And he told-- there was-- there was an office with at least a half a dozen-- all SS men in this Gestapo, all in SS uniform, except this guy, who was wearing civilian clothes.
And he said something to them.
I don't-- I was in a state of shock.
I don't know what-- what he said to them.
And I stood in that corner, maybe, for 45 minutes to an hour.
And they all took turns coming by with these heavy black boots, and they kicked me and beat me.
And I kept sagging down.
And-- and they just beat the hell out of me, so to say.
And all the time, when it-- every time somebody came by, they would say to me in German, [SPEAKING GERMAN], which means where is-- where is your father hiding his money?
That's all they wanted to know.
And they kept on beating me.
And then, one of them came over, and he looked at my hands, and he said, oh, you've never worked a day in your life.
I can see that by your hands.
We're going to teach you how to dig coal, how to carry bricks.
And all of this, you will learn at Dachau.
In those days, they were primarily deporting prisoners to Dachau.
He said, you are going to learn how to work, he said.
And all this went on, and they kept on beating me.
